figures.caption: Table 3. LiDAR ground segmentation on nuScenes [4] validation split. We train TerraSeg on OmniLiDAR using pseudo-labels from our PseudoLabeler. GndNet [31] is trained on the full nuScenes training split, i.e. no sub-sampling. The column Labels indicates whether manually labeled data is used for training. Prec. stands for precision. Best performance in bold, second-best is underlined.

Figure 4. Qualitative success cases on nuScenes dataset [4]. The top row shows ground truth labels, while the bottom row displays TerraSeg predictions. Ground points are color-coded by elevation; non-ground points are rendered in gray. From left to right, columns correspond to scan IDs 1787, 3238, and 3479.

Figure 5. Qualitative success cases on SemanticKITTI dataset [2]. The top row shows ground truth labels, while the bottom row displays TerraSeg predictions. Ground points are color-coded by elevation; non-ground points are rendered in gray. From left to right, columns correspond to scan IDs 4006, 2714, and 2816.

Figure 2. Overview of TerraSeg. (a) PseudoLabeler generates self-supervised point-wise ground/non-ground labels per raw LiDAR scan. (b) OmniLiDAR unifies 12 major automotive datasets within an aggregated framework, yielding a diverse curated corpus drawn from nearly 22 million raw scans. (c) TerraSeg is a real-time, domain-agnostic model for ground segmentation, trained on OmniLiDAR using these self-supervised pseudo-labels. We introduce two architectures: accurate Base model (TerraSeg-B) and efficient Small model (TerraSeg-S).

Table 4. LiDAR ground segmentation on SemanticKITTI [2] validation split. We train TerraSeg on OmniLiDAR using pseudo-labels from our PseudoLabeler. GndNet [31] is trained on the full SemanticKITTI training split, i.e. no sub-sampling. The column Labels indicates whether manually labeled data is used for training. Prec. stands for precision. Best performance in bold, second-best is underlined.

Table 5. LiDAR ground segmentation on Waymo Perception [37] validation split. We train TerraSeg on OmniLiDAR using pseudolabels from our PseudoLabeler. GndNet [31] is trained on the full Waymo training split, i.e. no sub-sampling. The column Labels indicates whether manually labeled data is used for training. Prec. stands for precision. Best performance in bold, second-best is underlined.

Table 9. Hardware diversity in OmniLiDAR. A detailed breakdown of the 15 unique LiDAR sensor models utilized across the datasets. The datasets cover a wide spectrum of sensor configurations, including classic spinning LiDARs, solid-state variants, and FMCW sensors, ensuring robust cross-sensor generalization during training.

Table 1. Overview of main methods for ground segmentation. In the Method type column, Handcrafted refers to rule-based, nonlearnable algorithms, while Supervised and Self-supervised indicate learning-based approaches. In the Tasks columns, Seg. and Elev. mean ground Segmentation and Elevation mapping, respectively. †Implemented in TorchRobotics [20]. ‡Confidence is bin-level only, used internally for classification, not output as a per-point score. §We obtained source code from the authors but the project is not open source.

Table 7. PseudoLabeler ablation study II. We ablate the pillar size vxy and recovery threshold τ for post-processing. We report mIoU on nuScenes (NS), SemanticKITTI (SK), and Waymo Perception (WP) validation splits. The column Avg indicates Average mIoU. Best performance in bold, second-best is underlined.

Table 2. OmniLiDAR dataset. We unify 12 major public autonomous driving datasets into a single standardized LiDAR format, enabling large-scale cross-dataset training and evaluation. We curate the LiDAR scans to obtain a diverse training set by downsampling the LiDAR sequences to 0.2Hz, i.e. one LiDAR scan every five seconds. During training, we mitigate dataset imbalance by sampling with datasetspecific probabilities (see Section 3.2). All reported statistics are computed over the available LiDAR data, as some datasets provide only partial sensor releases. †Two different sensor configurations were used for data collection; we report the total number of distinct LiDARs used across configurations. ‡Only 3D LiDAR sensors are considered. §Across all datasets, we identify 15 unique LiDAR hardware models.

Figure 8. Qualitative failure cases on SemanticKITTI dataset [2]. The top row shows ground truth labels, while the bottom row displays TerraSeg predictions. Ground points are color-coded by elevation; non-ground points are rendered in gray. From left to right, columns correspond to scan IDs 3300, 947, and 3173.

Figure 9. Qualitative failure cases on Waymo Perception dataset [37]. The top row shows ground truth labels, while the bottom row displays TerraSeg predictions. Ground points are color-coded by elevation; non-ground points are rendered in gray. From left to right, columns correspond to scan IDs 2513, 4755, and 2024.

Figure 3. Post-processing for pseudo-labeling. The point cloud is divided into pillars to recover misclassified non-ground points. Points within a vertical window defined by [ĥ − Hp1, ĥ + Hp2] around the estimated height ĥ = g(xi, yi) are analyzed: if a pillar contains both ground and non-ground points, all points above the pillar’s lowest point plus margin τ are reclassified as non-ground.

Figure 6. Qualitative success cases on Waymo Perception dataset [37]. The top row shows ground truth labels, while the bottom row displays TerraSeg predictions.
